channel.title: AI Firehose: What Is New
channel.description: We read the whole AI firehose so you do not have to, then distill it into one brief: what is new, what is breaking out, and what is worth your time, across the day, week, month, and quarter. You take what you want and leave the rest.

item.title: Meta head of product for 'AI for work' transformation is leaving company
item.description: donohoe: The head of product leading Meta's 'AI for work' transformation initiative is departing the company. This represents a notable shift in Meta's organizational structure around workplace AI efforts.

item.title: [x86] AI Compute Extensions (ACE) Specification
item.description: matt_d: x86 AI Compute Extensions (ACE) is a specification for CPU instruction set extensions designed to accelerate AI computations on x86 processors. The specification defines new instructions and capabilities for improving AI inference and training performance on standard CPUs.

item.title: How to turn off AI in your Google Docs
item.description: TechCrunch AI: TechCrunch provides guidance on disabling AI features in Google Docs, specifically the "write with Gemini" prompts that appear to users. The article offers instructions for turning off these AI-assisted writing suggestions.

item.title: Sovereign AI: Why Owning the Full Stack Is the New Strategic Imperative
item.description: HardAnchor: The author argues that vertical integration and full-stack ownership of AI infrastructure is becoming a strategic necessity. Control over chips, models, and deployment enables independence, security, and competitive advantage in the AI era.

item.title: Noam Shazeer is joining OpenAI
item.description: devanshp: Noam Shazeer, a prominent AI researcher previously at Google, is joining OpenAI. This move reflects competitive talent acquisition in the large language model space.

item.title: Noam Shazeer Joins OpenAI
item.description: lukasgross: Noam Shazeer has joined OpenAI. Shazeer previously founded Together AI and worked at Google Brain on large language models.

item.title: Inside India newsletter: Anthropic curbs ignite AI debate in India — efforts 'too slow, way too small' - CNBC
item.description: Google News AI: Anthropic has implemented operational curbs in India, sparking debate about the adequacy of India's AI governance response. Critics argue the nation's efforts to address these restrictions are insufficiently fast and too limited in scope.

item.title: GLM-5.2 is probably the most powerful text-only open weights LLM
item.description: Simon Willison: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, a 753B parameter text-only open weights LLM with 40 active parameters via Mixture of Experts and a 1 million token context window. It ranks first on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index and second on the Code Arena WebDev leaderboard, though it generates significantly more output tokens than comparable models.

item.title: Towards autonomous medical artificial intelligence agents - Nature
item.description: Google News AI: Nature publishes an article discussing the trajectory toward autonomous AI agents in medical applications. The piece appears to explore current progress and future directions for AI systems capable of autonomous decision-making and action in healthcare contexts.

item.title: French president urges US to share cutting-edge AI and democracies to cooperate on regulation - AP News
item.description: Google News AI: The French president called on the US to share advanced AI technology and advocated for democratic nations to collaborate on AI regulation. The statement reflects concerns about equitable access to AI capabilities and coordinated governance approaches among democracies.

item.title: Everything I Learned Training Frontier Small Models – Maxime Labonne, Liquid AI [video]
item.description: Topfi: Maxime Labonne from Liquid AI presents lessons learned from training frontier small models. The talk covers insights into the training methodology and best practices for developing efficient small language models.

item.title: Toward an O*NET for AI R&D
item.description: Epoch AI: Epoch AI proposes adapting the O*NET occupational classification system to create a systematic framework for tracking and categorizing AI research automation. This would enable structured measurement of which research tasks are being automated and at what scale.

item.title: A robot is sprinting towards you. Do you want it running on Claude or Grok?
item.description: Usu: A provocative question comparing Claude and Grok as foundation models for robot control. The framing suggests implicit debate about safety, reliability, or performance differences between the two systems in a high-stakes scenario.

item.title: Roelof Botha joins SpaceX’s board of directors
item.description: TechCrunch AI: Roelof Botha, former Sequoia Capital leader, joined SpaceX's board of directors, filling an existing vacancy days after SpaceX's IPO, which was noted as the largest IPO ever.

item.title: After unveiling ridiculously expensive AR glasses, Snap’s stock takes a dive
item.description: TechCrunch AI: Snap unveiled new AR glasses at a high price point, which was followed by a decline in the company's stock price. TechCrunch characterizes the glasses as "ridiculously expensive" and notes the negative market reaction to the product debut.

item.title: NEA’s Tiffany Luck says enterprises are still figuring out their AI ROI
item.description: TechCrunch AI: NEA investor Tiffany Luck discusses how enterprises are struggling to measure AI return on investment. After aggressive "tokenmaxxing" spending earlier in the year, companies like Uber exhausted budgets quickly, leading to cost constraints and pullbacks on AI tool licenses.

item.title: Agentic coding and persistent returns to expertise
item.description: droidjj: A discussion on Hacker News by droidjj examining how agentic coding systems interact with expert specialization. The piece explores the thesis that even as autonomous coding agents advance, deep expertise in specific domains continues to provide durable competitive advantages.

item.title: Every Level of Hermes Agent Explained
item.description: Jack Roberts: Jack Roberts breaks down a seven-level progression for building Hermes agents, from basic prompts to fully automated agentic operating systems. The video covers connecting multiple LLM providers (Claude, ChatGPT, Grok, DeepSeek) into unified workflows, building custom skills, orchestrating parallel sub-agents, and automating scheduled tasks.

item.title: The hacker sent by Anthropic to calm the government's nerves about AI safety
item.description: Brajeshwar: A commentary on Anthropic's effort to engage with government regarding AI safety concerns. The title's framing with "hacker sent by Anthropic" suggests skepticism about the motivations and effectiveness of the company's government relations strategy on safety issues.

item.title: World leaders want American AI. They just don’t want America to be able to turn it off.
item.description: TechCrunch AI: World leaders including French President Macron and Indian PM Modi raised concerns at the G7 summit that the U.S. could unilaterally cut off access to American AI systems. The Anthropic service outage made this vulnerability concrete for international stakeholders worried about dependence on U.S. AI infrastructure.

item.title: Converting SQL Server types and rules to PostgreSQL
item.description: Google Cloud Tech: Google Cloud's Database Migration Service enables developers to convert SQL Server schemas and code to PostgreSQL equivalents using Gemini as an AI tutor. The service assists with type conversions like SQL Server user-defined types to PostgreSQL DOMAINs while explaining the migration process.

item.title: Microsoft's launched OpenClaw?
item.description: Jack Roberts: Microsoft Scout is an AI agent integrated into the Microsoft ecosystem that proactively acts on user behalf across Teams, email, and calendar without waiting for explicit prompts. It automates task setup and preparation like gathering meeting materials based on plain language work descriptions.

item.title: Native Active Perception as Reasoning for Omni-Modal Understanding
item.description: Zhenghao Xing, Ruiyang Xu, Yuxuan Wang: OmniAgent is an omni-modal agent that formulates video understanding as a POMDP-based iterative Observation-Thought-Action cycle to reduce computational cost from video duration. It introduces Agentic Supervised Fine-Tuning via best-of-N trajectory synthesis and Agentic Reinforcement Learning with TAURA for turn-level credit assignment. The approach exhibits positive test-time scaling and achieves state-of-the-art results on video understanding benchmarks.

item.title: Beyond the Current Observation: Evaluating Multimodal Large Language Models in Controllable Non-Markov Games
item.description: Shengyuan Ding, Xilin Wei, Xinyu Fang: RNG-Bench is a benchmark suite that isolates multimodal language models' ability to reconstruct past observations and act on them in non-Markovian environments. It includes Matching Pairs and 3D Maze games with controlled difficulty axes, a head-to-head duel protocol, and a Memory Gap metric that separates forgetting from poor action selection.

item.title: Learning User Simulators with Turing Rewards
item.description: Yingshan Susan Wang, Cedegao E. Zhang, Linlu Qiu: Turing-RL is a reinforcement learning approach that trains user simulator LLMs to produce responses indistinguishable from real users by optimizing a discriminative Turing reward, rather than matching ground truth responses. The method is evaluated on conversational chat and Reddit forum discussion tasks, outperforming baselines on both LLM and human evaluation metrics.

item.title: Freeing the Law with LOCUS: A Local Ordinance Corpus for the United States
item.description: Denis Peskoff, Joe Barrow, Christopher Vu: LOCUS is a comprehensive machine-readable corpus of U.S. municipal and county ordinance codes covering 9,239 cities and counties. The authors use OCR to digitize fragmented local law documents and provide a harmonized access layer for research, along with ModernBERT-based classifiers trained to analyze ordinances across dimensions like opacity and paternalism.

item.title: 🔬 The Self-Driving Lab — Joseph Krause, Radical AI
item.description: Latent Space: Joseph Krause from Radical AI argues that in materials science, the sustainable competitive advantage lies in the laboratory infrastructure and processes rather than in the AI models themselves. The self-driving lab represents a moat based on physical capabilities and data generation rather than algorithmic superiority.

item.title: Do as I Do: Dexterous Manipulation Data from Everyday Human Videos
item.description: Bhawna Paliwal, Haritheja Etukuru, William Liang: DO AS I DO reconstructs hand-object interactions from monocular RGB human videos and retargets them to dexterous robotic manipulation trajectories. The method addresses the embodiment gap between human and robot hands to generate scalable robot manipulation training data from in-the-wild video sources.

item.title: Anthropic Employees Accuse Trump Administration of Targeting Them - The New York Times
item.description: Google News AI: Anthropic employees have accused the Trump Administration of targeting them, according to reporting by The New York Times. The report documents alleged political pressure on the AI company during the new administration.

item.title: The Chandra-Gaia Catalog of Counterparts: Resolving ambiguous Gaia matches to X-ray sources in the Chandra Source Catalog using Machine Learning
item.description: V. Samuel Pérez-Díaz, Vinay L. Kashyap, Joshua D. Ingram: The Chandra-Gaia Catalog of Counterparts uses a gradient-boosted classifier (LightGBM) trained on source properties to cross-match X-ray sources from the Chandra Source Catalog with optical sources from Gaia Data Release 3. The framework identifies true counterparts, detects chance coincidences, and resolves ambiguities for ~254k X-ray sources, with the machine-learning approach validated against the Chandra Orion Ultradeep Project.

item.title: UBP2: Uncertainty-Balanced Preference Planning for Efficient Preference-based Reinforcement Learning
item.description: Mohamed Nabail, Leo Cheng, Jingmin Wang: UBP2 is a model-based approach for preference-based reinforcement learning that actively directs exploration by jointly reasoning over uncertainties in reward, dynamics, and value functions using ensembles. The method achieves sublinear regret guarantees and demonstrates improved sample efficiency compared to model-free and non-optimistic baselines on Meta-World.

item.title: Rethinking Reward Supervision: Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation
item.description: Siyi Gu, Jialin Chen, Sophia Zhou: The paper proposes Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation, which uses structured rubric feedback instead of scalar rewards or noisy chain-of-thought annotations to train reasoning models. A two-stage pipeline first generates task-specific rubrics, then trains a student model conditioned on rubric-level criteria, achieving better performance on science reasoning benchmarks than GRPO and OPSD baselines.

item.title: Reference-Driven Multi-Speaker Audio Scene Generation from In-the-Wild Priors
item.description: Michael Finkelson, Daniel Segal, Eitan Richardson: ScenA is a technique for multi-speaker audio scene generation that conditions a text-to-audio flow-matching foundation model on multiple reference voices and natural language scene descriptions. It addresses the "Reference Shortcut" problem where models could identify speakers from acoustic similarity in noisy data through a high-noise-biased timestep distribution.

item.title: Data Intelligence Agents: Interpreting, Modeling, and Querying Enterprise Data via Autonomous Coding Agents
item.description: Anoushka Vyas, Aarushi Dhanuka, Sina Khoshfetrat Pakazad: Data Intelligence Agents (DIA) is a system of three autonomous coding agents that automate enterprise data discovery, schema creation, and SQL query generation. The agents generate executable artifacts, validate outputs, repair failures, and maintain shared memory for experience reuse. The system is deployed in production and achieves state-of-the-art results on seven SQL benchmarks.

item.title: Explaining Attention with Program Synthesis
item.description: Amiri Hayes, Belinda Li, Jacob Andreas: The paper presents a technique to explain attention heads in transformer models by generating executable Python programs that reproduce attention patterns. Programs are generated by prompting a language model with attention matrix summaries, then ranked and validated on held-out data. The approach successfully replaces up to 25% of attention heads with programmatic surrogates in GPT-2, TinyLlama, and Llama models with minimal performance degradation.

item.title: NeuMesh++: Towards Versatile and Efficient Volumetric Editing with Disentangled Neural Mesh-based Implicit Field
item.description: Chong Bao, Yuan Li, Bangbang Yang: NeuMesh++ is a mesh-based neural implicit representation that encodes disentangled geometry, texture, and semantic information on mesh vertices for 3D scene editing. It enables comprehensive editing functionalities including mesh-guided geometry editing, texture swapping, painting, and semantic-guided editing with enhanced rendering quality and training stability.

item.title: Diffusion-Proof: Recipe for Formal Theorem Proving Beyond Auto-Regressive Generation
item.description: Ruida Wang, Rui Pan, Pengcheng Wang: Diffusion-Proof is a framework for training diffusion language models (dLLMs) on formal theorem proving tasks. It includes dLLM-Prover-7B for whole-proof writing and dLLM-Corrector-7B, a block diffusion-based correction model that leverages bi-directional in-filling to perform local proof corrections. The approach outperforms auto-regressive baselines by 1.61% on ProofNet-Test and 6.14% on MiniF2F-Test, and solves an IMO problem that DeepSeek-Prover-V2-7B could not.

item.title: Using AI to improve a challenging reaction in medicinal chemistry
item.description: ilreb: A HackerNews post by ilreb describing how AI techniques were applied to optimize or improve a difficult chemical reaction in medicinal chemistry research. The work likely involved machine learning models trained on chemical data to guide synthesis strategy or reaction conditions.

item.title: Coding with ADK to beat a text adventure game
item.description: Google Cloud Tech: Google Cloud Tech hosted a livestream demonstrating how to use the Agent Development Kit (ADK) to solve a text adventure game. Speakers Tilde Thurium and Abdel Sghiouar showed practical applications of agent development through a game-based example.

item.title: Enhancing Decision-Making with Large Language Models through Multi-Agent Fictitious Play
item.description: Leyang Shen, Yang Zhang, Xiaoyan Zhao: Multi-Agent Fictitious Play (MAFP) is a paradigm for LLM-based multi-agent systems that treats decision-making as an equilibrium-seeking process. It addresses "stance entanglement" in tasks requiring simultaneous reasoning across mutually dependent stakeholder perspectives by iteratively updating agent decisions through best responses to empirical mixtures of past decisions.

item.title: Show HN: Relaymux, a tmux-based meta-harness for local coding agents
item.description: byhong03: Relaymux is a tmux-based orchestration harness designed for running local coding agents with minimal overhead. It enables interactive CLI agents to run in tmux windows while the orchestrator manages task tracking and communicates status via Telegram or iMessage.

item.title: P-K-GCN: Physics-augmented Koopman-enhanced Graph Convolutional Network for Deep Spatiotemporal Super-resolution
item.description: Xizhuo, Zhang, Zekai Wang: P-K-GCN combines graph convolutional networks with Koopman operator theory and physics-based loss regularization to perform spatiotemporal super-resolution on irregular geometries. The method reconstructs high-resolution spatiotemporal data from coarse-grained inputs while enforcing physical constraints. Theoretical analysis shows that physics augmentation and Koopman regularization reduce super-resolution error by tightening generalization bounds.

item.title: Optimal scenario design for climate emulation
item.description: Christopher B. Womack, Shahine Bouabid, Andrei Sokolov: Womack et al. present a method to optimize climate model training data by using a differentiable Simple Climate Model to iteratively update training scenarios based on emulator loss sensitivity. A single optimized scenario outperforms emulators trained on six standard ScenarioMIP pathways despite using less data, demonstrating that dataset design can be as important as architecture for surrogate models.

item.title: Confidence is Not Reliability: Rethinking MC Dropout in Brain Tumour Segmentation
item.description: Xin Ci Wong, Duygu Sarikaya, Kieran Zucker: This paper evaluates voxel-level uncertainty estimation via Monte Carlo Dropout for glioma segmentation in multiparametric MRI, testing whether MC Dropout can reliably identify segmentation errors in clinically critical brain tumor sub-regions. The study found that while global uncertainty-error alignment was strong (AUROC 0.97), model-specific calibration failures in critical sub-regions were invisible to standard metrics, demonstrating that sub-region-specific calibration assessment is necessa

item.title: Does VLA Even Know the Basics? Measuring Commonsense and World Knowledge Retention in Vision-Language-Action Models
item.description: Nikita Kachaev, Andrey Moskalenko, Matvey Skripkin: Act2Answer is a lightweight evaluation protocol that adapts VLM knowledge benchmarks to Vision-Language-Action models by requiring agents to answer questions through robotic actions. The method uses action-grounded object placement tasks to measure commonsense and world knowledge retention while reducing control confounds. Layerwise intent probing is applied to localize answer-relevant information across VLA architectures.

item.title: Context intelligence for your data and AI agents at scale - Amazon Web Services
item.description: Google News AI: Amazon Web Services announced a context intelligence capability for data and AI agents at scale. The service appears designed to help manage and organize contextual information for AI agent applications in enterprise environments.

item.title: Risk Stratification for ICU Delirium using Pervasive Ambient Sensing Information
item.description: Jiaqing Zhang, Sabyasachi Bandyopadhyay, Miguel Contreras: A study evaluates sequential neural network models trained on ambient sound and light data from 309 ICU patients to predict delirium across multiple time horizons. Sound features emerged as dominant predictors, with convolutional models achieving AUC of 0.80, and Shapley analysis used for interpretability.

item.title: Quoting Charity Majors
item.description: Simon Willison: Charity Majors argues that 2025 inverted the economics of code production, making code generation nearly free and instant rather than expensive and time-consuming. This shift transforms code from carefully curated and reused assets into disposable, regenerable artifacts, requiring stronger engineering discipline to manage increased complexity.

item.title: Only 16 Percent of Americans Think AI Will Have a Positive Impact on Society
item.description: karakoram: A Hacker News discussion of survey data showing only 16 percent of Americans hold a positive view of AI's societal impact. This reflects broader public skepticism toward AI adoption and outcomes.

item.title: Correct Yourself, Keep My Trust: How Self-Correction and Social Connection Shape Credibility in Social Chatbots
item.description: Biswadeep Sen, Yi-Chieh Lee: A between-subjects experiment (N=120) tested three error correction strategies for social chatbots: webpage retraction, self-correction, and expert chatbot correction. Self-correction maintained credibility without damage while all three strategies corrected errors equally well. Social connection with the chatbot significantly predicted belief change only when the chatbot corrected itself, not when corrections came from external sources.
